title: System Clock Skew — TPM Attestation and ESP Timeout Risk
description: The device's real-time clock deviates from NTP by more than two minutes. Microsoft documents that a clock off by several minutes causes TPM attestation errors (self-deploying/pre-provisioning) and ESP timeouts. Knowledge source: docs/agent/autopilot-ztd-diagnostics.md.
author: Autopilot Monitor
explanation:
The device's system clock is off by **{{offsetSeconds}} seconds** relative to the NTP reference (time.windows.com). Microsoft documents that Autopilot provisioning can fail with TPM attestation errors or ESP timeouts when the real-time clock is off by several minutes — certificate and token validation are time-sensitive.

This is especially critical for **self-deploying and pre-provisioning** deployments (TPM attestation) and can also skew all client-side timestamps in this session's timeline.

remediation.title: Sync the clock before provisioning
steps: Boot the device to the start of OOBE and establish a network connection.
Run 'w32tm /resync /force' (Shift+F10 command prompt) to sync with time.windows.com.
Ensure UDP port 123 to time.windows.com is open in the deployment network.
Check the BIOS/RTC battery and firmware clock if the skew recurs on the same hardware.

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Windows Autopilot known issues — device provisioning can fail (clock skew)
